author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Pretty cool, I have tried this before but with a laser printer, epic fail.
I was wondering if when you get the air bubbles, could you prick them with a pin before getting the card to scape the air out? Just a thought as it works with other types of applications like wallpaper etc.
Great looking result, hope the present is well received, home made gifts are always better than store bought.

author: @TheIrvy
text: Thanks for showing your "working out", a lot of people might have edited that out.  If I could add my suggestion, you can buy iron transfer paper for inkjet printers, you could print the image on that and then iron the print onto the wood and then seal it with the varnish.  However, this method wouldn't have the same distressed look you get from removing the paper with the toothbrush, and I like that effect.  It's a great idea with a lot of potential, thanks for sharing it.

author: @Lossanaght
text: Might be worth trying that process with a overhead transparency in stead of paper. I'd bet id could be peeled off without leaving any residue. Alternatively you might try coating the paper with a water soluble glue like PVA and letting it dry before printing, so the ink doesn't soak into the paper as much.

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text: *Hi Luke* thats interesting... I remember back in the day we used to lift layers of photo prints off and press it onto to cups etc. You can also put a photo print on to glasses, or a mirror. If i remember correctly it didnt involve other chemicals and was simply submerging the photo print in hot water, got that soaking until the layer was free and sliding. Try it with a spare 6,4 and see what happens. All the best, kieron

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Wow, that sounds super awesome! I might have to give this ago because I wanted to do something with glass printing for a while. Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @DiyEcoProjects
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey  @Luke Neame  :) if it doesnt work, remind me and ill try and find out what I did. It was like the layer of emulsion the picture was sitting on removed itself from the backing layer - was see through and gummy. That stuck on the glass, bowl, whatever by itself. Havent seen it on wood before, so that was interesting. Have fun

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: I did this using spray lacquer on the painting and then one the board, worked ok, but I found that using a light spray on photo paper, then printing the image on top, then spraying the board and sticking the parer down, leaving it to dry completely than soaking it till the paper lifted.  This was the best result I got, but it was years ago when you could still get lacquer that was 100% waterproof.  Not sure some of these modern water based paints would work. Still, you used water based varnish so it might still work.  I wonder why you made it print onto wood, then put it in a picture frame.  I would have had the wood all varnished to shine up so everyone knew it was printed on wood.
